structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
The partial document is a structured guide titled **“The Complete Guide to Building Skills for Claude.”** It outlines the core sections covered:

- **Introduction** – an opening overview of the guide’s purpose and scope.  
- **Fundamentals** – basic concepts and foundational knowledge needed to create Claude skills.  
- **Planning and Design** – strategies for outlining, architecting, and designing effective skill workflows.  
- **Testing and Iteration** – methods for validating skill behavior, debugging, and refining through iterative cycles.  
- **Distribution and Sharing** – guidance on packaging, deploying, and sharing completed skills with users or marketplaces.  
- **Patterns and Troubleshooting** – common design patterns, best‑practice solutions, and troubleshooting tips for recurring issues.  
- **Resources and References** – curated links, tools, and further reading to support ongoing skill development.  

Overall, the guide aims to provide a comprehensive, step‑by‑step framework for building, testing, and deploying Claude skills, supplemented with practical patterns and resource material.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: This introductory excerpt explains that “skills” are modular folders of instructions that teach Claude specific tasks or workflows, allowing users to avoid repeating preferences, processes, and domain knowledge. It highlights the benefits of skills for repeatable workflows—such as frontend design generation, research, document creation, and multi‑step orchestration—and notes their synergy with Claude’s built‑in features (code execution, document creation) and MCP integrations. The guide promises to cover everything needed to build effective skills, including technical requirements, best‑practice patterns for both standalone and MCP‑enhanced workflows, testing, iteration, and distribution. It identifies the target audience (developers, power users, and teams) and outlines two learning paths—standalone skill development or MCP‑enhanced integration—while emphasizing shared technical foundations. Readers are told they can create a functional skill in 15‑30 minutes using the skill‑creator, and the document proceeds to Chapter 1 on fundamentals.

structure.title: Fundamentals
structure.summary:
The excerpt introduces the concept of “skills” as modular folders containing a required SKILL.md with YAML front‑matter, optional scripts, references, and assets. It outlines three core design principles:

* **Progressive Disclosure** – a three‑level loading system (YAML front‑matter always in the system prompt, the full SKILL.md body loaded when relevant, and additional linked files accessed on demand) to minimize token usage while preserving expertise.  
* **Composability** – skills must function alongside other skills without assuming exclusivity.  
* **Portability** – a skill created once works across Claude.ai, Claude Code, and the API, provided dependencies are met.

The text then explains the role of skills in conjunction with MCP (Connectors), using a kitchen analogy: MCP supplies tools and infrastructure, while skills provide the “recipes” (workflows and best practices). It contrasts scenarios without skills—users face unclear next steps, support tickets, inconsistent results—and with skills—pre‑built, consistent workflows, lower learning curves, and reliable tool usage. The section ends by transitioning to the next chapter on planning and design.

structure.title: Planning and design
structure.summary: The excerpt from Chapter 2 outlines how to plan and design an Anthropic skill. It begins with a method for defining concrete use cases, provides a detailed sprint‑planning example, and categorizes common skill use‑cases into Document & Asset Creation, Workflow Automation, and MCP Enhancement. It then explains how to establish success criteria using quantitative metrics (trigger rate, tool‑call count, API‑error rate) and qualitative metrics (user‑prompt independence, workflow consistency). Technical requirements follow, covering the required file structure, naming conventions, and the mandatory YAML front‑matter in SKILL.md, along with security restrictions on prohibited content. The document offers guidance on writing effective skill descriptions—what the skill does, when to use it, and trigger phrases—with examples of good and bad descriptions, and details the template for the main instructions, examples, and troubleshooting sections. Best‑practice recommendations emphasize specificity, error handling, and progressive disclosure. The section concludes by introducing the next chapter on testing and iteration.

structure.title: Testing and iteration
structure.summary: The passage is Chapter 3, “Testing and iteration,” which outlines how to validate and refine Claude AI skills. It describes three testing rigor levels—manual testing in Claude.ai, scripted testing in Claude Code, and programmatic testing via the skills API—helping readers pick the right method for their skill’s visibility and quality needs. A “Pro Tip” advises focusing on a single challenging task first, then expanding to broader test coverage. The chapter presents a recommended testing framework divided into three areas: (1) triggering tests to ensure the skill activates on intended phrases and not on unrelated queries; (2) functional tests to verify correct outputs, API calls, error handling, and edge‑case behavior; and (3) performance comparisons that demonstrate the skill’s efficiency gains over a baseline. It introduces the “skill‑creator” tool for generating, reviewing, and iteratively improving skill definitions, including automated suggestions for triggers, test cases, and common issue flagging. Finally, it stresses ongoing iteration based on feedback, addressing under‑triggering, over‑triggering, and execution problems, and hints at the upcoming Chapter 4 on distribution and sharing.

structure.title: Distribution and sharing
structure.summary: The excerpt is from Chapter 4, “Distribution and Sharing.” It explains how adding skills enhances MCP integration, outlines how individual users and administrators obtain and deploy skills (download, zip, upload via Claude.ai settings or place in the Claude Code directory; organization‑wide deployment with automatic updates), and introduces the open Agent Skills standard for cross‑platform portability. It details programmatic access through a Skills API (endpoints, container.skills parameter, version control, SDK integration) and contrasts when to use the API versus the Claude.ai UI (ad‑hoc vs. production use). The section then recommends a workflow for publishing skills on GitHub (public repo, clear README, examples), linking them in MCP documentation, and providing a step‑by‑step installation guide. Finally, it gives guidance on positioning a skill—focusing on outcomes, emphasizing the combined value of MCP + skills, and using effective messaging in READMEs and marketing.

structure.title: Patterns and troubleshooting
structure.summary: The excerpt is Chapter 5 of a guide on building and maintaining “skills” that orchestrate MCP (Managed Cloud Platform) tools. It begins by contrasting problem‑first and tool‑first design mindsets, then presents five reusable patterns—sequential workflow orchestration, multi‑MCP coordination, iterative refinement, context‑aware tool selection, and domain‑specific intelligence—each with use‑case examples, step‑by‑step structures, and key techniques. The second half is a troubleshooting handbook that walks readers through common skill‑upload errors (missing or malformed SKILL.md, invalid front‑matter, illegal skill names), activation problems (skill not triggering or triggering too often), MCP connection failures, instruction‑following issues, and performance degradations caused by large context sizes, offering concrete diagnostics and remediation steps. The section concludes by segueing into Chapter 6, which lists resources and references.

structure.title: Resources and references
structure.summary:
Chapter 6 provides a curated set of resources for creating and publishing Claude AI “skills.” It starts by advising new developers to follow the Best Practices Guide and consult the API reference, then lists official documentation (Best Practices Guide, Skills Documentation, API Reference, MCP Documentation) and relevant blog posts that explain skill concepts and creation. A public GitHub repository (anthropics/skills) supplies example skills for customization.  

The chapter introduces the built‑in **skill‑creator** tool (available in Claude.ai and Claude Code) for generating, reviewing, and validating skills via simple prompts. It outlines support channels: community Discord for general questions and GitHub Issues for bug reports, with guidance on what information to include.  

A quick‑check checklist (Reference A) helps developers validate their skill before and after upload, covering use‑case identification, folder structure, required files (SKILL.md, YAML front‑matter), naming conventions, description quality, error handling, testing, and post‑upload monitoring.  

Reference B details the required and optional fields of the YAML front‑matter, naming rules, metadata options, and security restrictions (no XML tags, safe YAML parsing, reserved name prefixes).  

Reference C points to complete, production‑ready skill examples—document‑generation skills, workflow patterns, and partner integrations (e.g., Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Zapier)—hosted in up‑to‑date repositories for cloning and adaptation.
